articles.headline: India Issues Flood Advisory to Pakistan, Marking First Official Contact Since May Conflict
articles.explainer:
• India's Ministry of External Affairs issued an official flood advisory to Pakistan regarding water levels in the Chenab River basin.
• This marks the first formal government-to-government contact between the two nations since the 2025 India-Pakistan conflict in May.
• The advisory, facilitated through established Indus Waters Treaty mechanisms, highlights the importance of water management cooperation despite political tensions.
• Experts suggest this limited engagement could potentially pave the way for de-escalation or future dialogues on other pressing issues.
• Pakistan acknowledged the advisory, indicating adherence to the bilateral treaty protocols.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora
prelimsPoints: Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) signed in 1960, brokered by the World Bank.
IWT allocates eastern rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) to India and western rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) to Pakistan.
The treaty includes provisions for data exchange on river flows and flood warnings.
The current advisory concerns the Chenab River, a western river, underscoring IWT's functional importance.
The Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) is the bilateral mechanism for implementing the treaty.
mainsPoints: The incident underscores the resilience and utility of functional cooperation mechanisms like the Indus Waters Treaty even amidst severe political and military tensions.
It highlights the humanitarian imperative of disaster management transcending national boundaries and political animosity.
Discuss the potential for 'track-two' diplomacy or issue-based cooperation to build trust and de-escalate conflicts between adversarial nations.
Analyze the role of international treaties in maintaining a semblance of stability in volatile regions.
Examine the long-term implications of such limited engagements on the broader India-Pakistan relationship and regional stability.
articles.imageDescription: Map showing the Indus River System and the distribution of rivers under the Indus Waters Treaty.
articles.trivia: The Indus Waters Treaty is often cited as one of the most successful water-sharing treaties in the world, having survived multiple conflicts between India and Pakistan.

articles.headline: RBI Holds Repo Rate Steady, Prioritizes Inflation Control Amidst Global Headwinds
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) concluded its bi-monthly review, deciding to maintain the repo rate at its current level.
• The decision was unanimous, signaling a continued focus on bringing inflation within the target band, despite nascent signs of economic recovery.
• Governor Shaktikanta Das emphasized vigilance against global commodity price volatility and geopolitical uncertainties impacting domestic inflation.
• Projections for Q2 GDP growth were slightly revised downwards, while inflation forecasts for the fiscal year remained largely unchanged.
• The RBI reiterated its commitment to supporting growth while ensuring price stability, maintaining an 'withdrawal of accommodation' stance.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Monetary policy
prelimsPoints: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) consists of six members: three from the RBI and three external members appointed by the government.
The primary objective of monetary policy in India is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
The current inflation target band is 4% with a +/- 2% tolerance.
Repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks; reverse repo is the rate at which it borrows from banks.
The MPC meets at least four times a year.
mainsPoints: Analyze the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control and growth stimulus in the current global economic scenario.
Discuss the effectiveness of interest rate tools in managing supply-side inflation pressures.
Evaluate the implications of a 'withdrawal of accommodation' stance on different sectors of the Indian economy (e.g., credit growth, investment, consumption).
Examine the role of forward guidance and communication in shaping market expectations and monetary policy transmission.
Assess how geopolitical events and global commodity prices complicate domestic monetary policy decisions.
articles.imageDescription: Reserve Bank of India building in Mumbai.
articles.trivia: The first Governor of the Reserve Bank of India was Sir Osborne Smith, while the first Indian Governor was C.D. Deshmukh.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Key Provisions of Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark ruling, largely upheld the constitutional validity of several key provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023.
• Petitions challenging the Act primarily focused on concerns regarding state surveillance powers and exemptions granted to government entities.
• The Court emphasized the balance between the right to privacy and legitimate state interests, urging for strict adherence to established safeguards.
• It directed the government to establish the Data Protection Board within the stipulated timeframe and ensure its operational independence.
• The ruling provides clarity and strengthens the legal framework for data privacy in India, impacting technology companies and citizens alike.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Cyber security
prelimsPoints: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, was enacted to provide for the processing of digital personal data in a manner that recognizes both the right of individuals to protect their personal data and the need to process personal data for lawful purposes.
It replaces the IT Rules 2011 on data protection.
Key principles include purpose limitation, data minimization, and accountability.
The Act proposes a Data Protection Board of India as an independent regulatory body.
Significant categories: 'Data Principal' (individual whose data is processed) and 'Data Fiduciary' (entity determining means of processing).
mainsPoints: Analyze the implications of the Supreme Court's verdict on the balance between individual privacy rights and state surveillance capabilities under the DPDP Act.
Discuss the challenges in implementing the DPDP Act, particularly for small and medium enterprises (SMEs) and the burgeoning Indian tech sector.
Evaluate the effectiveness of the Data Protection Board as an independent regulatory body and its potential role in safeguarding citizen data.
Compare and contrast India's data protection framework with global standards like GDPR, highlighting strengths and weaknesses.
Examine the impact of this ruling on the future of digital governance and the digital economy in India.
articles.imageDescription: Supreme Court of India building.
articles.trivia: The Puttaswamy judgment (2017) declared the right to privacy as a fundamental right under Article 21 of the Indian Constitution, laying the groundwork for data protection legislation.

prelimsSyllabus.topic: Right to Privacy and Data Protection

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Launches Advanced Earth Observation Satellite for Ocean Monitoring
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched a new-generation Earth Observation Satellite (EOS) from Sriharikota.
• The satellite, named EOS-X, is designed to provide high-resolution data for oceanography, coastal zone management, and climate studies.
• Equipped with advanced synthetic aperture radar (SAR) and multi-spectral sensors, it will enhance India's capability in marine forecasting.
• This mission is a crucial step towards strengthening India's climate resilience and disaster management infrastructure.
• The satellite has been placed into a sun-synchronous polar orbit, and initial data reception has been confirmed.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Awareness in the fields of Space
prelimsPoints: ISRO's primary launch vehicle for Earth Observation Satellites is the Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle (PSLV).
Earth Observation Satellites provide data for various applications like agriculture, forestry, urban planning, and disaster monitoring.
SAR technology allows for imaging through clouds and at night, crucial for weather-independent observations.
Sun-synchronous orbit ensures that the satellite passes over any given point of the Earth's surface at the same local solar time.
India has a robust constellation of remote sensing satellites, contributing to global efforts in climate monitoring.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of advanced Earth Observation Satellites for India's economic development and national security.
Analyze how data from EOS-X can contribute to India's efforts in combating climate change and enhancing disaster preparedness, particularly for coastal regions.
Examine the role of indigenous space technology in achieving self-reliance and fostering scientific innovation in India.
Evaluate the potential applications of high-resolution oceanographic data for sectors such as fisheries, shipping, and offshore energy exploration.
Highlight the international collaborations and partnerships that can be strengthened through India's advancements in space technology.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic representation of an Earth Observation Satellite orbiting Earth.
articles.trivia: India's first satellite, Aryabhata, was launched in 1975 by the Soviet Union. The first indigenous launch vehicle was SLV-3, launched in 1979.

articles.headline: Government Unveils Ambitious New Targets for National Green Hydrogen Mission by 2030
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy announced enhanced targets for the National Green Hydrogen Mission, aiming for significant production capacity by 2030.
• The revised goals focus on rapidly scaling up green hydrogen production to reduce carbon emissions and achieve energy independence.
• Key incentives include expanded financial support for electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production facilities.
• The government also outlined plans for developing green hydrogen export hubs and establishing a robust regulatory framework.
• This initiative is central to India's commitment to achieving net-zero emissions by 2070 and positioning itself as a global leader in green energy.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: Green hydrogen is produced by electrolysis of water using renewable energy sources.
The National Green Hydrogen Mission was approved in January 2023.
The mission aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production, utilization, and export.
Key components include Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition (SIGHT) programme for electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production.
Other types of hydrogen include Grey (from natural gas), Blue (from natural gas with CCS), and Pink (from nuclear energy).
mainsPoints: Discuss the economic and environmental benefits of accelerating green hydrogen adoption in India, particularly for hard-to-abate sectors like steel and fertilizers.
Analyze the challenges in achieving the ambitious green hydrogen targets, including infrastructure development, cost reduction, and technological advancements.
Evaluate the potential of India to become a global leader in green hydrogen production and export, considering its renewable energy potential and strategic location.
Examine the policy support and financial incentives required to attract investment and foster innovation in the green hydrogen ecosystem.
Assess the role of green hydrogen in India's broader energy transition strategy and its commitment to international climate goals.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized image showing green hydrogen production from renewable energy.
articles.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, making up roughly 75% of all baryonic mass.

articles.headline: India Hosts BIMSTEC Foreign Ministers' Meeting, Focus on Enhancing Regional Connectivity and Trade
articles.explainer:
• India hosted the BIMSTEC (Bay of Bengal Initiative for Multi-Sectoral Technical and Economic Cooperation) Foreign Ministers' Meeting in New Delhi.
• The discussions centered on strengthening regional connectivity, boosting trade and investment, and fostering cooperation in disaster management and climate resilience.
• Member states emphasized the need for expediting the implementation of the BIMSTEC Master Plan for Transport Connectivity.
• India reaffirmed its commitment to the 'Neighborhood First' policy and highlighted its role in promoting regional integration.
• A joint statement was issued, outlining commitments to deepen cooperation across various sectors and prepare for the upcoming BIMSTEC Summit.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
India and its neighborhood relations
prelimsPoints: BIMSTEC comprises seven member states: Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, Myanmar, Nepal, Sri Lanka, and Thailand.
It was established in 1997 with the aim of fostering economic and technical cooperation among its members.
The Bay of Bengal is a crucial geopolitical and geoeconomic space for India.
BIMSTEC focuses on 14 priority sectors including trade, technology, energy, transport, tourism, and fisheries.
India sees BIMSTEC as a vital platform for its 'Act East' and 'Neighborhood First' policies, especially given the challenges faced by SAARC.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic significance of BIMSTEC for India's foreign policy, particularly in the context of its 'Act East' and 'Neighborhood First' initiatives.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities in enhancing regional connectivity and trade within the BIMSTEC framework.
Evaluate BIMSTEC's potential as an alternative or complementary regional grouping to SAARC, considering their respective strengths and weaknesses.
Examine the role of multi-sectoral cooperation within BIMSTEC in addressing common challenges such as climate change, disaster management, and maritime security.
Suggest policy recommendations for strengthening BIMSTEC to achieve its full potential for regional integration and prosperity.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of BIMSTEC member countries.
articles.trivia: The BIMSTEC Permanent Secretariat is located in Dhaka, Bangladesh, established in 2014.

articles.headline: Government Expands Production Linked Incentive (PLI) Scheme to Boost Advanced Manufacturing
articles.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet approved the expansion of the Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme to include new sectors under advanced manufacturing.
• The new sectors are expected to attract significant domestic and foreign investment, fostering self-reliance and job creation.
• The objective is to enhance India's manufacturing capabilities and integrate it further into global supply chains for high-tech products.
• The scheme offers incentives on incremental sales from products manufactured in India, encouraging local production and value addition.
• This move aligns with the 'Make in India' initiative and aims to boost India's competitiveness in key strategic industries.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Industrial policy
prelimsPoints: The PLI scheme was introduced in 2020 to boost domestic manufacturing and make India a global manufacturing hub.
It provides incentives to companies for enhancing their domestic manufacturing, which in turn leads to job creation and economic growth.
Currently, the scheme covers 14 key sectors, including electronics, automobiles, pharmaceuticals, textiles, and telecom equipment.
The incentives are usually calculated as a percentage of incremental sales over a base year.
The scheme aims to reduce India's reliance on imports and promote export-oriented manufacturing.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the effectiveness of the PLI scheme in transforming India into a global manufacturing hub and attracting foreign direct investment (FDI).
Discuss the challenges faced by Indian industries in competing with established global players in advanced manufacturing sectors, and how PLI addresses them.
Analyze the potential impact of expanded PLI on job creation, skill development, and technological upgradation in India.
Examine the role of government incentives in fostering self-reliance and reducing import dependence in critical sectors.
Suggest measures to ensure that the PLI scheme promotes sustainable and inclusive industrial growth, avoiding undue concentration of benefits.
articles.imageDescription: A factory floor with advanced machinery.
articles.trivia: The 'Make in India' initiative was launched in 2014 to encourage companies to manufacture their products in India.

articles.headline: NITI Aayog Releases Comprehensive AI Ethics Framework for Responsible AI Development
articles.explainer:
• NITI Aayog published its much-anticipated Artificial Intelligence Ethics Framework, outlining guidelines for responsible AI development and deployment.
• The framework emphasizes principles of fairness, transparency, accountability, and privacy in AI systems across various sectors.
• It calls for a multi-stakeholder approach, involving government, industry, academia, and civil society, in shaping India's AI future.
• The guidelines aim to prevent algorithmic bias, ensure data security, and promote human oversight in critical AI applications.
• This initiative positions India at the forefront of global efforts to establish ethical standards for emerging technologies.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights
Cyber security
prelimsPoints: NITI Aayog is the premier policy 'Think Tank' of the Government of India, providing directional and policy inputs.
AI ethics frameworks typically address issues like fairness, privacy, security, transparency, and accountability.
Algorithmic bias refers to systematic and repeatable errors in a computer system that create unfair outcomes.
India aims to leverage AI for inclusive growth and societal benefit, outlined in its 'AI for All' strategy.
Global efforts for AI ethics include initiatives by UNESCO, OECD, and the European Union.
mainsPoints: Discuss the critical ethical considerations that arise with the increasing adoption of Artificial Intelligence across various sectors in India.
Analyze how NITI Aayog's AI Ethics Framework addresses concerns related to algorithmic bias, data privacy, and accountability, and its potential impact on AI development.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing ethical AI principles in practice, particularly in areas like facial recognition, healthcare diagnostics, and autonomous systems.
Examine the role of multi-stakeholder collaboration in ensuring responsible AI innovation and governance in a democratic society.
Suggest policy measures beyond the framework that India could adopt to foster a robust and ethical AI ecosystem, including regulatory and educational initiatives.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized image of interconnected nodes representing AI and ethics.
articles.trivia: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy in 1956 at the Dartmouth Conference.

articles.headline: Parliament Passes Amendments to Juvenile Justice Act, Focusing on Rehabilitation and Child Protection
articles.explainer:
• Both Houses of Parliament passed significant amendments to the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015.
• The amendments aim to strengthen the provisions for child protection, rehabilitation, and social reintegration of children in conflict with the law.
• Key changes include enhanced accountability for district authorities and a clearer framework for adoption procedures.
• The bill also addresses concerns regarding the definition of 'serious offenses' and the role of Juvenile Justice Boards.
• Child rights organizations have largely welcomed the amendments, emphasizing the need for robust implementation mechanisms.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
keyTerms: Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015
Children in need of care and protection (CNCP)
prelimsPoints: The Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015, replaced the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2000.
It allows for juveniles aged 16-18 years to be tried as adults in cases of heinous crimes.
Key bodies under the Act are the Juvenile Justice Boards (JJBs) for children in conflict with law and Child Welfare Committees (CWCs) for children in need of care and protection.
The amendments likely focus on strengthening these bodies and ensuring faster adoption processes.
The Act is aligned with the United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child (UNCRC).
mainsPoints: Analyze how the recent amendments to the Juvenile Justice Act aim to strike a balance between punitive measures for serious offenses and the rehabilitative ethos of juvenile justice.
Discuss the challenges in ensuring effective implementation of child protection and rehabilitation programs under the amended Act, especially at the district level.
Evaluate the role of various stakeholders, including the police, judiciary, child welfare committees, and civil society organizations, in upholding child rights under the JJ Act.
Examine the sociological factors contributing to juvenile delinquency in India and how legislative reforms can address these root causes.
Suggest further reforms or policy interventions needed to create a more robust and child-friendly juvenile justice system in India.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration of children and legal scales, representing juvenile justice.
articles.trivia: India ratified the United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child (UNCRC) in 1992.

articles.headline: New Report Highlights Increasing Frequency and Intensity of Extreme Weather Events in India
articles.explainer:
• A recent report released by the Indian Meteorological Department (IMD) and a leading environmental think tank detailed a worrying trend of extreme weather events.
• The report indicates a significant increase in the frequency and intensity of heatwaves, heavy rainfall, droughts, and cyclones across various regions of India.
• It attributes these changes primarily to anthropogenic climate change and highlights the vulnerability of India's agricultural sector and coastal communities.
• The findings underscore the urgent need for enhanced climate adaptation strategies, early warning systems, and resilient infrastructure.
• Policy recommendations include greater investment in renewable energy, sustainable land use practices, and community-level preparedness.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Disaster and disaster management
prelimsPoints: The Indian Meteorological Department (IMD) is the principal agency responsible for meteorological observations, weather forecasting, and seismology in India.
Extreme weather events are becoming more frequent globally due to climate change.
India's long coastline makes it particularly vulnerable to cyclones and sea-level rise.
The National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) is the apex body for disaster management in India.
Climate adaptation involves adjusting to actual or expected future climate, while mitigation involves reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
mainsPoints: Analyze the multi-faceted impacts of increasing extreme weather events on India's economy, society, and environment.
Discuss the effectiveness of current disaster management policies and early warning systems in India in light of the new report's findings.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for India in building climate-resilient infrastructure and promoting sustainable agricultural practices.
Examine the role of international cooperation and technology transfer in helping India address the escalating threats posed by climate change.
Suggest comprehensive policy frameworks that integrate climate mitigation, adaptation, and disaster risk reduction strategies at national and local levels.
articles.imageDescription: Satellite image showing a severe weather pattern over the Indian subcontinent.
articles.trivia: The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) is the UN body for assessing the science related to climate change.

articles.headline: India-USA Strategic Dialogue Concludes, Reinforcing Indo-Pacific Cooperation and Tech Partnerships
articles.explainer:
• The annual India-USA Strategic Dialogue concluded in Washington D.C., with a focus on deepening cooperation in the Indo-Pacific region.
• Discussions covered shared security interests, defense partnerships, and economic collaboration, particularly in critical and emerging technologies.
• Both nations reiterated their commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific and discussed strategies to counter regional challenges.
• Agreements were reached on enhancing cooperation in space exploration, cybersecurity, and supply chain resilience.
• The dialogue underscored the growing convergence of strategic interests between India and the United States.
gsSubTopics: Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
prelimsPoints: The India-USA strategic partnership has evolved significantly, encompassing defense, trade, technology, and people-to-people ties.
The Indo-Pacific concept emphasizes a free, open, and rules-based order in the region.
The Quad (Quadrilateral Security Dialogue) includes India, USA, Japan, and Australia, focusing on regional security and cooperation.
Critical and Emerging Technologies (CET) dialogue aims to enhance collaboration in areas like AI, quantum computing, and semiconductors.
Defense cooperation includes joint military exercises and technology transfer initiatives.
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolving nature of the India-USA strategic partnership and its implications for India's foreign policy and strategic autonomy.
Discuss India's role in the Indo-Pacific region and how cooperation with the USA contributes to regional stability and security.
Evaluate the significance of technology partnerships, particularly in critical and emerging technologies, for India's economic growth and national security.
Examine the challenges and opportunities in balancing India's multi-alignment strategy with its deepening ties with the United States.
Assess how bilateral cooperation can address common challenges such as climate change, global health, and counter-terrorism.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and the USA side-by-side.
articles.trivia: The 'Malabar' naval exercise is a trilateral/quadrilateral exercise involving India, USA, Japan, and Australia (since 2020).

articles.headline: Government Boosts Digital Public Infrastructure for Enhanced Financial Inclusion and Service Delivery
articles.explainer:
• The government announced new initiatives to expand India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) to further enhance financial inclusion and streamline public service delivery.
• Focus areas include increasing the penetration of Aadhaar, UPI, and the Open Network for Digital Commerce (ONDC) in remote areas.
• Training programs for digital literacy and financial awareness are being scaled up to ensure equitable access.
• The aim is to leverage technology to bridge the digital divide and bring more citizens into the formal economy.
• This expansion is crucial for India's goal of becoming a trillion-dollar digital economy.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) refers to shared digital systems that are foundational for public and private sector innovation.
India's DPI stack includes Aadhaar (digital identity), UPI (real-time payments), and DigiLocker (digital document wallet).
The JAM Trinity (Jan Dhan-Aadhaar-Mobile) has been instrumental in expanding financial inclusion.
ONDC (Open Network for Digital Commerce) aims to democratize e-commerce by shifting from a platform-centric model to an open network.
Financial inclusion refers to providing affordable and accessible financial services to all segments of society.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative potential of India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) in achieving comprehensive financial inclusion and efficient public service delivery.
Discuss the challenges associated with expanding DPI, including digital literacy gaps, cybersecurity risks, and ensuring equitable access across diverse demographics.
Evaluate the role of Aadhaar, UPI, and ONDC in empowering citizens and fostering economic growth, particularly for small businesses and informal sector workers.
Examine the 'India Stack' model as a unique approach to digital governance and its potential for replication in other developing countries.
Suggest policy measures to further strengthen DPI, ensuring data privacy, robust security, and continued innovation while addressing potential monopolistic tendencies.
articles.imageDescription: Infographic showing interconnected digital services.
articles.trivia: UPI processed over 10 billion transactions in a single month for the first time in August 2023.

articles.headline: Electoral Reforms Committee Submits Report with Key Recommendations to Government
articles.explainer:
• A high-level committee constituted by the government to study electoral reforms submitted its comprehensive report.
• The report includes recommendations on campaign finance, voter registration, inner-party democracy, and the use of technology in elections.
• It proposes measures to curb the influence of money and muscle power, ensuring free and fair elections.
• The committee also addressed issues related to the Model Code of Conduct and the adjudication of election disputes.
• The government is expected to review the recommendations for potential legislative changes in the upcoming parliamentary session.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Appointment to various Constitutional posts, powers, functions and responsibilities of various Constitutional Bodies
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act
prelimsPoints: The Election Commission of India (ECI) is an autonomous constitutional authority responsible for administering election processes in India.
Article 324 of the Constitution provides for the Election Commission.
Major electoral reforms previously suggested include banning of criminal candidates, state funding of elections, and electronic voting machine (EVM) reforms.
The Representation of the People Act, 1951, governs the conduct of elections in India.
Model Code of Conduct (MCC) is a set of guidelines issued by the ECI for political parties and candidates during elections to ensure free and fair elections.
mainsPoints: Analyze the persistent challenges in India's electoral system that necessitate comprehensive reforms, such as campaign finance transparency and criminalization of politics.
Discuss the potential impact of the committee's recommendations on strengthening democratic processes, ensuring level playing field, and enhancing voter trust.
Evaluate the feasibility and implications of proposed reforms like state funding of elections and mandatory inner-party elections.
Examine the role of the Election Commission of India (ECI) in implementing electoral reforms and upholding the sanctity of the electoral process.
Suggest how technology can be leveraged more effectively and securely to improve electoral integrity and voter participation, addressing concerns like EVM transparency.
articles.imageDescription: A ballot box with a hand inserting a vote.
articles.trivia: The first general elections in independent India were held from 1951 to 1952.

articles.headline: Conservation Efforts Intensify for Critically Endangered Great Indian Bustard Amidst Habitat Loss
articles.explainer:
• Conservationists and government agencies are stepping up efforts to protect the critically endangered Great Indian Bustard (GIB).
• The initiatives focus on habitat restoration, captive breeding programs, and mitigation of threats from power lines and agricultural expansion.
• A recent survey highlighted the dwindling population, primarily in Rajasthan and Gujarat, underscoring the urgency of the situation.
• The Supreme Court has previously intervened, directing measures to protect the bird, including burying power lines.
• Collaborative efforts involving local communities are crucial for the long-term survival of this iconic species.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Biodiversity
prelimsPoints: The Great Indian Bustard (GIB) is listed as 'Critically Endangered' on the IUCN Red List.
It is protected under Schedule I of the Wildlife Protection Act, 1972.
Its primary habitats are grasslands and scrublands in Rajasthan, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Karnataka, and Andhra Pradesh.
Major threats include habitat loss, power lines, hunting, and agricultural intensification.
Project Great Indian Bustard was launched by the Rajasthan government, and the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change also supports conservation efforts.
mainsPoints: Analyze the ecological significance of the Great Indian Bustard as a flagship species for grassland ecosystems and the broader implications of its extinction.
Discuss the multi-pronged approach required for the effective conservation of critically endangered species, including habitat protection, community engagement, and policy interventions.
Evaluate the challenges posed by infrastructure development (e.g., power lines, renewable energy projects) to wildlife conservation, particularly for species like the GIB.
Examine the role of judicial intervention in environmental protection and the implementation of conservation directives.
Suggest sustainable development models that can balance economic growth with biodiversity conservation in fragile ecosystems.
articles.imageDescription: A Great Indian Bustard in its natural grassland habitat.
articles.trivia: The Great Indian Bustard is the state bird of Rajasthan.

articles.headline: Skill India Mission 3.0 Launched with Focus on Future-Ready Skills and Green Jobs
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship launched Skill India Mission 3.0, building on previous phases with new objectives.
• The renewed mission emphasizes imparting future-ready skills, including digital literacy, AI, robotics, and sustainable practices for green jobs.
• It aims to align skill development programs with industry demands and emerging economic opportunities.
• A strong focus is placed on enhancing employability, promoting entrepreneurship, and integrating vocational training with formal education.
• The initiative seeks to empower India's youth and leverage its demographic dividend for economic growth.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
prelimsPoints: Skill India Mission was launched in 2015 to train over 40 crore people in India in different skills by 2022.
It includes various initiatives like Pradhan Mantri Kaushal Vikas Yojana (PMKVY), National Skill Development Mission, and National Policy for Skill Development and Entrepreneurship.
The National Skill Development Corporation (NSDC) is a not-for-profit public limited company under the Ministry of Skill Development & Entrepreneurship.
Green jobs are jobs that help protect ecosystems and biodiversity, reduce energy and water consumption, decarbonize the economy, and minimize waste.
India has a significant demographic dividend, with a large working-age population.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of Skill India Mission 3.0 in addressing the evolving skill gaps in India's workforce and preparing youth for future employment opportunities.
Discuss the challenges in effectively implementing skill development programs, including issues of quality assurance, industry linkage, and geographical reach.
Evaluate the potential of 'green jobs' and future-ready skills to drive sustainable economic growth and address environmental concerns in India.
Examine how integrating vocational training with formal education can enhance the employability of youth and reduce the 'degree vs. skill' dilemma.
Suggest policy recommendations to ensure that skill development initiatives are inclusive, cater to marginalized sections, and leverage technology for wider outreach.
articles.imageDescription: Young people learning new skills in a vocational training center.
articles.trivia: India is projected to have the world's largest working-age population by 2030.

articles.headline: National Health Mission Review Highlights Progress and Gaps in Rural Healthcare Delivery
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare released its annual review of the National Health Mission (NHM).
• The report noted significant progress in maternal and child health indicators and the expansion of primary healthcare facilities in rural areas.
• However, it also identified persistent gaps in human resources, infrastructure, and equitable access to specialized care, particularly in remote regions.
• The review emphasized the need for greater investment in public health infrastructure and strengthening of Ayushman Bharat-Health and Wellness Centres.
• Recommendations include targeted interventions for vulnerable populations and leveraging digital health solutions to improve service delivery.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
articles.source: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
prelimsPoints: The National Health Mission (NHM) was launched in 2013 by subsuming the National Rural Health Mission (NRHM) and the National Urban Health Mission (NUHM).

Its main programmatic components include health system strengthening in rural and urban areas, reproductive-maternal-neonatal-child and adolescent health (RMNCH+A), and communicable and non-communicable diseases.
Ayushman Bharat comprises two components: Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (PMJAY) and Health and Wellness Centres (HWCs).
HWCs aim to provide comprehensive primary health care (CPHC) closer to the community.
India's healthcare spending as a percentage of GDP is relatively low compared to developed nations.
mainsPoints: Analyze the achievements and persistent challenges in India's rural healthcare delivery system under the National Health Mission (NHM).
Discuss the role of Ayushman Bharat-Health and Wellness Centres (HWCs) in transforming primary healthcare and promoting preventive health.
Evaluate the impact of human resource shortages and infrastructure deficits on healthcare outcomes in remote and underserved areas.
Examine how digital health solutions and telemedicine can bridge gaps in healthcare access and improve service efficiency.
Suggest policy recommendations to strengthen public health infrastructure, ensure equitable access to quality healthcare, and address health disparities across states.
articles.imageDescription: A rural health center with doctors and patients.
articles.trivia: The 'Janani Shishu Suraksha Karyakram' (JSSK) is a government initiative to reduce maternal and infant mortality.

articles.headline: India Crosses Major Milestone in Solar Energy Capacity, Boosting Renewable Targets
articles.explainer:
• India achieved a significant milestone by crossing a major threshold in its installed solar energy capacity, reinforcing its commitment to renewable energy.
• The achievement is attributed to robust policy support, declining solar panel costs, and increased private sector investment.
• This surge in solar power generation is crucial for meeting India's ambitious renewable energy targets and reducing reliance on fossil fuels.
• The government highlighted the success of schemes like the PM-KUSUM and solar park development in driving this growth.
• The focus now shifts to grid integration challenges and energy storage solutions to ensure reliable power supply.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
prelimsPoints: India's current target is to achieve 500 GW of non-fossil fuel energy capacity by 2030.
The International Solar Alliance (ISA) was jointly launched by India and France in 2015.
PM-KUSUM scheme aims to provide financial and water security to farmers by enabling them to install solar pumps and solar power plants on their barren land.
Solar power is a crucial component of India's climate commitments under the Paris Agreement.
Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) and Photovoltaic (PV) are two main technologies for harnessing solar energy.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to India's rapid growth in solar energy capacity and its implications for energy security and climate goals.
Discuss the challenges associated with large-scale integration of intermittent renewable energy sources like solar into the national grid.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government schemes and policies in promoting solar energy adoption, particularly in rural and agricultural sectors.
Examine the potential of energy storage technologies (e.g., battery storage, pumped hydro) in enhancing the reliability and dispatchability of solar power.
Suggest policy recommendations to further accelerate India's transition to a renewable energy-dominated future, considering economic viability and environmental sustainability.
articles.imageDescription: A large solar power farm under a clear sky.
articles.trivia: Bhadla Solar Park in Rajasthan is one of the world's largest solar power plants.

articles.headline: Review of National Education Policy Implementation Reveals Mixed Progress
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education released a review report on the implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020.
• The report highlighted commendable progress in certain areas, such as curriculum reforms and digital learning initiatives.
• However, it also pointed out challenges in teacher training, infrastructure development, and ensuring equitable access, especially in higher education.
• The review emphasized the need for greater state-level coordination and increased budgetary allocation for education.
• Recommendations include accelerating the establishment of multidisciplinary institutions and strengthening vocational education pathways.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
prelimsPoints: The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 replaced the 1986 National Policy on Education.
It aims for universalization of education from pre-school to secondary level and focuses on holistic, multidisciplinary education.
Key reforms include a new 5+3+3+4 curricular structure, emphasis on foundational literacy and numeracy, and flexibility in subject choices.
The policy targets 50% Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in higher education by 2035.
It advocates for increased public investment in education to 6% of GDP.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative vision of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 and its potential to revolutionize India's education system.
Discuss the major achievements and significant challenges encountered during the initial years of NEP implementation, particularly in primary and higher education sectors.
Evaluate the effectiveness of curriculum reforms, multidisciplinary approaches, and digital learning initiatives in enhancing learning outcomes and employability.
Examine the role of state governments and private institutions in achieving the goals of NEP, considering disparities in resources and priorities.
Suggest concrete measures to address the identified gaps in teacher training, infrastructure, and equitable access to ensure inclusive and quality education for all.
articles.imageDescription: Students in a modern classroom setting.
articles.trivia: The Kothari Commission (1964-66) recommended increasing education expenditure to 6% of GDP, a target reiterated by NEP 2020.

articles.headline: Panel Recommends Greater Financial Autonomy for Urban Local Bodies to Boost Development
articles.explainer:
• A government-appointed expert panel submitted its recommendations advocating for greater financial autonomy and resource mobilization for Urban Local Bodies (ULBs).
• The report highlighted the critical role of ULBs in driving urban development and service delivery but noted their dependence on state and central grants.
• Recommendations include empowering ULBs to levy and collect more local taxes, access municipal bonds, and leverage public-private partnerships.
• The panel also suggested capacity building for ULB officials in financial management and urban planning.
• This move aims to strengthen decentralization and improve the quality of life in India's rapidly urbanizing cities.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies.
prelimsPoints: The 74th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992, gave constitutional status to Urban Local Bodies (ULBs).
ULBs are responsible for providing basic civic amenities and governance in urban areas.
Sources of municipal finance include property tax, user charges, grants from state and central governments, and market borrowings (municipal bonds).
The 12th Schedule of the Constitution lists 18 functions to be performed by municipalities.
Financial dependence on state governments is a major challenge for ULBs.
mainsPoints: Analyze the current financial challenges faced by Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) in India and their impact on urban development and service delivery.
Discuss how greater financial autonomy for ULBs, as recommended by the expert panel, can strengthen decentralization and local governance.
Evaluate the potential of municipal bonds and other innovative financing mechanisms in mobilizing resources for urban infrastructure projects.
Examine the role of capacity building and good governance practices in improving the efficiency and accountability of ULBs.
Suggest comprehensive reforms, including legislative and administrative changes, to empower ULBs to become self-sufficient and responsive to urban needs.
articles.imageDescription: A modern city skyline representing urban development.
articles.trivia: The first municipal corporation in India was established in Madras (Chennai) in 1688.

articles.headline: India's Tourism Sector Shows Robust Recovery, But Faces New Challenges
articles.explainer:
• A report by the Ministry of Tourism highlighted a strong rebound in India's tourism sector, with increasing domestic and international tourist arrivals.
• Government initiatives like 'Dekho Apna Desh' and improved connectivity have contributed significantly to this recovery.
• However, the sector faces new challenges including sustainable tourism practices, infrastructure bottlenecks in remote areas, and skilled labor shortages.
• The report emphasized the need for promoting responsible tourism, leveraging digital platforms, and investing in niche tourism segments.
• Tourism is a key contributor to India's GDP and employment, and its sustained growth is vital for economic recovery.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
prelimsPoints: The 'Dekho Apna Desh' initiative was launched to encourage citizens to travel within the country.
India aims to become a major global tourism destination.
Tourism sector contributes significantly to GDP and employment generation.
Challenges include infrastructure, connectivity, and promotion of unexplored destinations.
Medical tourism and eco-tourism are growing niche segments in India.
mainsPoints: Analyze the socio-economic significance of the tourism sector for India and the factors driving its post-pandemic recovery.
Discuss the challenges faced by India's tourism industry, particularly in terms of infrastructure, sustainability, and skilled workforce.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government initiatives and promotional campaigns in boosting both domestic and international tourism.
Examine the concept of sustainable tourism and how it can be integrated into India's tourism development strategies to protect cultural and natural heritage.
Suggest policy measures to enhance India's competitiveness as a global tourism destination, including investment in niche segments and digital marketing.
articles.imageDescription: Tourists visiting a historical monument in India.
articles.trivia: The Ministry of Tourism launched the 'Incredible India' campaign to promote tourism globally.

articles.headline: Indian Startup Ecosystem Expands Beyond Metros to Tier-2 and Tier-3 Cities
articles.explainer:
• A new report indicates a significant surge in startup activity in India's Tier-2 and Tier-3 cities, diversifying the ecosystem beyond major metropolitan centers.
• This growth is fueled by improved digital infrastructure, government support for local entrepreneurship, and reverse migration of skilled talent.
• Startups in these cities are often focused on solving local problems, leveraging regional resources, and catering to specific market needs.
• Government schemes like 'Startup India' and incubation centers are playing a crucial role in nurturing this new wave of entrepreneurs.
• This decentralization of the startup ecosystem promises more inclusive economic growth and job creation across the country.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: The 'Startup India' initiative was launched in 2016 to build a strong ecosystem for nurturing innovation and startups in the country.
It offers various benefits like tax exemptions, easier compliance, and funding support.
Tier-2 and Tier-3 cities are experiencing growth due to lower operational costs, access to local talent, and government incentives.
Incubation centers and accelerators play a vital role in providing mentorship, infrastructure, and funding access to early-stage startups.
India is home to a large number of unicorns (startups valued at over $1 billion).
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to the expansion of India's startup ecosystem into Tier-2 and Tier-3 cities and its significance for inclusive economic development.
Discuss the unique opportunities and challenges faced by startups operating in non-metro regions, including access to funding, mentorship, and skilled workforce.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government policies and initiatives, such as 'Startup India', in fostering entrepreneurship and innovation beyond major urban centers.
Examine how the decentralization of the startup ecosystem can lead to job creation, local problem-solving, and reverse migration of talent.
Suggest policy interventions and infrastructure development required to further strengthen the startup ecosystem in Tier-2 and Tier-3 cities, ensuring sustainable growth.
articles.imageDescription: A group of young entrepreneurs collaborating in a co-working space.
articles.trivia: Bengaluru is often referred to as the 'Silicon Valley of India' due to its vibrant tech and startup scene.

highlights: India and Pakistan re-establish official contact with a flood advisory, first since May 2025 conflict, via Indus Waters Treaty mechanisms.
RBI holds repo rate steady, prioritizing inflation control amidst global economic uncertainties; forecasts for Q2 GDP revised downwards.
Supreme Court upholds key provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, strengthening India's data privacy framework.
ISRO successfully launches EOS-X, an advanced Earth Observation Satellite, enhancing capabilities in ocean monitoring and climate studies.
Government unveils ambitious new targets for the National Green Hydrogen Mission by 2030, aiming for global leadership in green energy.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT), consider the following statements:
1. The Treaty was signed in 1960 and brokered by the World Bank.
2. It allocates the western rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) to India and eastern rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) to Pakistan.
3. The Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) is the bilateral mechanism for implementing the treaty.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: The Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) was signed in 1960 and brokered by the World Bank. Statement 2 is incorrect: The IWT allocates the eastern rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) to India and western rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) to Pakistan. Statement 3 is correct: The Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) is the bilateral mechanism for implementing the treaty.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) stack?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Aadhaar (digital identity), UPI (real-time payments), and ONDC (open e-commerce network) are key components of India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) stack. The National Stock Exchange (NSE) is a stock exchange, not part of the DPI stack.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the Great Indian Bustard (GIB):
1. It is listed as 'Critically Endangered' on the IUCN Red List.
2. It is protected under Schedule I of the Wildlife Protection Act, 1972.
3. Its primary habitats are dense forests in the Himalayan foothills.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: The Great Indian Bustard (GIB) is indeed listed as 'Critically Endangered' on the IUCN Red List. Statement 2 is correct: It is protected under Schedule I of the Wildlife Protection Act, 1972, providing it the highest level of protection. Statement 3 is incorrect: Its primary habitats are grasslands and scrublands, not dense forests in the Himalayan foothills.

prelimsMocks.question: The 'withdrawal of accommodation' stance by the Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) primarily implies:
options: (a) A focus on increasing liquidity in the financial system.
(b) A shift towards easing interest rates to stimulate growth.
(c) Prioritizing inflation control by reducing excess liquidity.
(d) Suspending all monetary policy operations temporarily.
prelimsMocks.answer: (c) Prioritizing inflation control by reducing excess liquidity.
prelimsMocks.explanation: The 'withdrawal of accommodation' stance means the MPC intends to gradually remove the accommodative monetary policy measures put in place during periods of economic slowdown. This implies prioritizing inflation control by reducing excess liquidity in the system, which typically involves higher interest rates or maintaining them at elevated levels.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following bodies is responsible for administering election processes in India as per Article 324 of the Constitution?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Article 324 of the Indian Constitution provides for the establishment and powers of the Election Commission of India (ECI), which is an autonomous constitutional authority responsible for administering election processes in India.

mainsMocks.question: Despite political tensions, functional cooperation mechanisms like the Indus Waters Treaty continue to operate. Discuss the significance of such 'track-two' diplomacy in de-escalating conflicts and fostering regional stability, with specific reference to India-Pakistan relations.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: India and its neighborhood relations; Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the India-Pakistan flood advisory as an example of functional cooperation. Explain the Indus Waters Treaty and its resilience. Discuss the concept of 'track-two' diplomacy and its role in building trust, maintaining communication channels, and addressing shared challenges (e.g., water, disaster management) even during conflict. Analyze how such engagements can prevent escalation and create space for future dialogue. Conclude with the broader implications for regional stability and the limitations of such engagements without political will.

mainsMocks.question: India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) has been lauded for its potential to drive financial inclusion and efficient service delivery. Critically examine the challenges in expanding DPI to remote areas and ensuring equitable access, and suggest measures to overcome the digital divide.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment; Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
mainsMocks.approach: Begin by defining DPI and highlighting its benefits (Aadhaar, UPI, ONDC) for financial inclusion. Then, discuss challenges in expansion: digital literacy gaps, language barriers, internet connectivity in remote areas, cybersecurity concerns, data privacy issues, and affordability of devices. Suggest measures: targeted digital literacy programs, multi-lingual interfaces, infrastructure investment (BharatNet), public-private partnerships, robust data protection laws, and subsidies for digital tools. Conclude by emphasizing the need for inclusive design and implementation to truly bridge the digital divide.

mainsMocks.question: The increasing frequency and intensity of extreme weather events pose a significant threat to India's development trajectory. Analyze the multi-faceted impacts of these events on key sectors of the Indian economy and society, and evaluate the effectiveness of current disaster management strategies. What comprehensive measures are needed to enhance India's climate resilience?
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment; Disaster and disaster management.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the report on extreme weather events and attribute them to climate change. Discuss impacts: agriculture (crop failure, food security), infrastructure (damage to roads, power), health (heat strokes, vector-borne diseases), economy (losses, insurance burden), displacement. Evaluate current disaster management (NDMA, early warning systems, NDRF) – highlighting strengths and weaknesses. Propose comprehensive measures for climate resilience: robust early warning systems, climate-resilient infrastructure, sustainable agriculture practices, ecosystem restoration, community-based disaster preparedness, renewable energy transition, and international cooperation. Conclude with the urgency of integrated strategies.

quickBytes.text: The Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) was signed in 1960, brokered by the World Bank.
Green Hydrogen is produced by electrolysis of water using renewable energy sources.
The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) in India has six members: three from RBI and three external.
The Right to Privacy was declared a fundamental right under Article 21 by the Puttaswamy judgment (2017).
BIMSTEC comprises 7 member states: Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, Myanmar, Nepal, Sri Lanka, and Thailand.
The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, replaced the IT Rules 2011 on data protection.
The Great Indian Bustard (GIB) is listed as 'Critically Endangered' on the IUCN Red List.
The 74th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992, gave constitutional status to Urban Local Bodies (ULBs).
NITI Aayog is the premier policy 'Think Tank' of the Government of India.
India's target is to achieve 500 GW of non-fossil fuel energy capacity by 2030.

GS2.headline: India Issues Flood Advisory to Pakistan, Marking First Official Contact Since May Conflict
GS2.explainer:
• India's Ministry of External Affairs issued an official flood advisory to Pakistan regarding water levels in the Chenab River basin.
• This marks the first formal government-to-government contact between the two nations since the 2025 India-Pakistan conflict in May.
• The advisory, facilitated through established Indus Waters Treaty mechanisms, highlights the importance of water management cooperation despite political tensions.
• Experts suggest this limited engagement could potentially pave the way for de-escalation or future dialogues on other pressing issues.
• Pakistan acknowledged the advisory, indicating adherence to the bilateral treaty protocols.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora
prelimsPoints: Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) signed in 1960, brokered by the World Bank.
IWT allocates eastern rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) to India and western rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) to Pakistan.
The treaty includes provisions for data exchange on river flows and flood warnings.
The current advisory concerns the Chenab River, a western river, underscoring IWT's functional importance.
The Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) is the bilateral mechanism for implementing the treaty.
mainsPoints: The incident underscores the resilience and utility of functional cooperation mechanisms like the Indus Waters Treaty even amidst severe political and military tensions.
It highlights the humanitarian imperative of disaster management transcending national boundaries and political animosity.
Discuss the potential for 'track-two' diplomacy or issue-based cooperation to build trust and de-escalate conflicts between adversarial nations.
Analyze the role of international treaties in maintaining a semblance of stability in volatile regions.
Examine the long-term implications of such limited engagements on the broader India-Pakistan relationship and regional stability.
GS2.imageDescription: Map showing the Indus River System and the distribution of rivers under the Indus Waters Treaty.
GS2.trivia: The Indus Waters Treaty is often cited as one of the most successful water-sharing treaties in the world, having survived multiple conflicts between India and Pakistan.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Key Provisions of Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark ruling, largely upheld the constitutional validity of several key provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023.
• Petitions challenging the Act primarily focused on concerns regarding state surveillance powers and exemptions granted to government entities.
• The Court emphasized the balance between the right to privacy and legitimate state interests, urging for strict adherence to established safeguards.
• It directed the government to establish the Data Protection Board within the stipulated timeframe and ensure its operational independence.
• The ruling provides clarity and strengthens the legal framework for data privacy in India, impacting technology companies and citizens alike.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Cyber security
prelimsPoints: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, was enacted to provide for the processing of digital personal data in a manner that recognizes both the right of individuals to protect their personal data and the need to process personal data for lawful purposes.
It replaces the IT Rules 2011 on data protection.
Key principles include purpose limitation, data minimization, and accountability.
The Act proposes a Data Protection Board of India as an independent regulatory body.
Significant categories: 'Data Principal' (individual whose data is processed) and 'Data Fiduciary' (entity determining means of processing).
mainsPoints: Analyze the implications of the Supreme Court's verdict on the balance between individual privacy rights and state surveillance capabilities under the DPDP Act.
Discuss the challenges in implementing the DPDP Act, particularly for small and medium enterprises (SMEs) and the burgeoning Indian tech sector.
Evaluate the effectiveness of the Data Protection Board as an independent regulatory body and its potential role in safeguarding citizen data.
Compare and contrast India's data protection framework with global standards like GDPR, highlighting strengths and weaknesses.
Examine the impact of this ruling on the future of digital governance and the digital economy in India.
GS2.trivia: The Puttaswamy judgment (2017) declared the right to privacy as a fundamental right under Article 21 of the Indian Constitution, laying the groundwork for data protection legislation.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Polity
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Right to Privacy and Data Protection

GS2.headline: India Hosts BIMSTEC Foreign Ministers' Meeting, Focus on Enhancing Regional Connectivity and Trade
GS2.explainer:
• India hosted the BIMSTEC (Bay of Bengal Initiative for Multi-Sectoral Technical and Economic Cooperation) Foreign Ministers' Meeting in New Delhi.
• The discussions centered on strengthening regional connectivity, boosting trade and investment, and fostering cooperation in disaster management and climate resilience.
• Member states emphasized the need for expediting the implementation of the BIMSTEC Master Plan for Transport Connectivity.
• India reaffirmed its commitment to the 'Neighborhood First' policy and highlighted its role in promoting regional integration.
• A joint statement was issued, outlining commitments to deepen cooperation across various sectors and prepare for the upcoming BIMSTEC Summit.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
India and its neighborhood relations
prelimsPoints: BIMSTEC comprises seven member states: Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, Myanmar, Nepal, Sri Lanka, and Thailand.
It was established in 1997 with the aim of fostering economic and technical cooperation among its members.
The Bay of Bengal is a crucial geopolitical and geoeconomic space for India.
BIMSTEC focuses on 14 priority sectors including trade, technology, energy, transport, tourism, and fisheries.
India sees BIMSTEC as a vital platform for its 'Act East' and 'Neighborhood First' policies, especially given the challenges faced by SAARC.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic significance of BIMSTEC for India's foreign policy, particularly in the context of its 'Act East' and 'Neighborhood First' initiatives.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities in enhancing regional connectivity and trade within the BIMSTEC framework.
Evaluate BIMSTEC's potential as an alternative or complementary regional grouping to SAARC, considering their respective strengths and weaknesses.
Examine the role of multi-sectoral cooperation within BIMSTEC in addressing common challenges such as climate change, disaster management, and maritime security.
Suggest policy recommendations for strengthening BIMSTEC to achieve its full potential for regional integration and prosperity.
GS2.trivia: The BIMSTEC Permanent Secretariat is located in Dhaka, Bangladesh, established in 2014.

GS2.headline: Parliament Passes Amendments to Juvenile Justice Act, Focusing on Rehabilitation and Child Protection
GS2.explainer:
• Both Houses of Parliament passed significant amendments to the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015.
• The amendments aim to strengthen the provisions for child protection, rehabilitation, and social reintegration of children in conflict with the law.
• Key changes include enhanced accountability for district authorities and a clearer framework for adoption procedures.
• The bill also addresses concerns regarding the definition of 'serious offenses' and the role of Juvenile Justice Boards.
• Child rights organizations have largely welcomed the amendments, emphasizing the need for robust implementation mechanisms.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
keyTerms: Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015
Children in need of care and protection (CNCP)
prelimsPoints: The Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015, replaced the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2000.
It allows for juveniles aged 16-18 years to be tried as adults in cases of heinous crimes.
Key bodies under the Act are the Juvenile Justice Boards (JJBs) for children in conflict with law and Child Welfare Committees (CWCs) for children in need of care and protection.
The amendments likely focus on strengthening these bodies and ensuring faster adoption processes.
The Act is aligned with the United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child (UNCRC).
mainsPoints: Analyze how the recent amendments to the Juvenile Justice Act aim to strike a balance between punitive measures for serious offenses and the rehabilitative ethos of juvenile justice.
Discuss the challenges in ensuring effective implementation of child protection and rehabilitation programs under the amended Act, especially at the district level.
Evaluate the role of various stakeholders, including the police, judiciary, child welfare committees, and civil society organizations, in upholding child rights under the JJ Act.
Examine the sociological factors contributing to juvenile delinquency in India and how legislative reforms can address these root causes.
Suggest further reforms or policy interventions needed to create a more robust and child-friendly juvenile justice system in India.
GS2.imageDescription: Illustration of children and legal scales, representing juvenile justice.
GS2.trivia: India ratified the United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child (UNCRC) in 1992.

GS2.headline: India-USA Strategic Dialogue Concludes, Reinforcing Indo-Pacific Cooperation and Tech Partnerships
GS2.explainer:
• The annual India-USA Strategic Dialogue concluded in Washington D.C., with a focus on deepening cooperation in the Indo-Pacific region.
• Discussions covered shared security interests, defense partnerships, and economic collaboration, particularly in critical and emerging technologies.
• Both nations reiterated their commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific and discussed strategies to counter regional challenges.
• Agreements were reached on enhancing cooperation in space exploration, cybersecurity, and supply chain resilience.
• The dialogue underscored the growing convergence of strategic interests between India and the United States.
gsSubTopics: Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
prelimsPoints: The India-USA strategic partnership has evolved significantly, encompassing defense, trade, technology, and people-to-people ties.
The Indo-Pacific concept emphasizes a free, open, and rules-based order in the region.
The Quad (Quadrilateral Security Dialogue) includes India, USA, Japan, and Australia, focusing on regional security and cooperation.
Critical and Emerging Technologies (CET) dialogue aims to enhance collaboration in areas like AI, quantum computing, and semiconductors.
Defense cooperation includes joint military exercises and technology transfer initiatives.
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolving nature of the India-USA strategic partnership and its implications for India's foreign policy and strategic autonomy.
Discuss India's role in the Indo-Pacific region and how cooperation with the USA contributes to regional stability and security.
Evaluate the significance of technology partnerships, particularly in critical and emerging technologies, for India's economic growth and national security.
Examine the challenges and opportunities in balancing India's multi-alignment strategy with its deepening ties with the United States.
Assess how bilateral cooperation can address common challenges such as climate change, global health, and counter-terrorism.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and the USA side-by-side.
GS2.trivia: The 'Malabar' naval exercise is a trilateral/quadrilateral exercise involving India, USA, Japan, and Australia (since 2020).

GS2.headline: Electoral Reforms Committee Submits Report with Key Recommendations to Government
GS2.explainer:
• A high-level committee constituted by the government to study electoral reforms submitted its comprehensive report.
• The report includes recommendations on campaign finance, voter registration, inner-party democracy, and the use of technology in elections.
• It proposes measures to curb the influence of money and muscle power, ensuring free and fair elections.
• The committee also addressed issues related to the Model Code of Conduct and the adjudication of election disputes.
• The government is expected to review the recommendations for potential legislative changes in the upcoming parliamentary session.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Appointment to various Constitutional posts, powers, functions and responsibilities of various Constitutional Bodies
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act
prelimsPoints: The Election Commission of India (ECI) is an autonomous constitutional authority responsible for administering election processes in India.
Article 324 of the Constitution provides for the Election Commission.
Major electoral reforms previously suggested include banning of criminal candidates, state funding of elections, and electronic voting machine (EVM) reforms.
The Representation of the People Act, 1951, governs the conduct of elections in India.
Model Code of Conduct (MCC) is a set of guidelines issued by the ECI for political parties and candidates during elections to ensure free and fair elections.
mainsPoints: Analyze the persistent challenges in India's electoral system that necessitate comprehensive reforms, such as campaign finance transparency and criminalization of politics.
Discuss the potential impact of the committee's recommendations on strengthening democratic processes, ensuring level playing field, and enhancing voter trust.
Evaluate the feasibility and implications of proposed reforms like state funding of elections and mandatory inner-party elections.
Examine the role of the Election Commission of India (ECI) in implementing electoral reforms and upholding the sanctity of the electoral process.
Suggest how technology can be leveraged more effectively and securely to improve electoral integrity and voter participation, addressing concerns like EVM transparency.
GS2.imageDescription: A ballot box with a hand inserting a vote.
GS2.trivia: The first general elections in independent India were held from 1951 to 1952.

GS2.headline: Skill India Mission 3.0 Launched with Focus on Future-Ready Skills and Green Jobs
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship launched Skill India Mission 3.0, building on previous phases with new objectives.
• The renewed mission emphasizes imparting future-ready skills, including digital literacy, AI, robotics, and sustainable practices for green jobs.
• It aims to align skill development programs with industry demands and emerging economic opportunities.
• A strong focus is placed on enhancing employability, promoting entrepreneurship, and integrating vocational training with formal education.
• The initiative seeks to empower India's youth and leverage its demographic dividend for economic growth.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
prelimsPoints: Skill India Mission was launched in 2015 to train over 40 crore people in India in different skills by 2022.
It includes various initiatives like Pradhan Mantri Kaushal Vikas Yojana (PMKVY), National Skill Development Mission, and National Policy for Skill Development and Entrepreneurship.
The National Skill Development Corporation (NSDC) is a not-for-profit public limited company under the Ministry of Skill Development & Entrepreneurship.
Green jobs are jobs that help protect ecosystems and biodiversity, reduce energy and water consumption, decarbonize the economy, and minimize waste.
India has a significant demographic dividend, with a large working-age population.
